extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a lab‑scale gantry crane that mimics harbor cranes at the port of Antwerp. It consists of a steel gantry structure with a hoist driven by a stepper motor controlled by a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller, a crane controller board, and sensors that report position and load. The crane moves small pucks that represent containers from one location to another. The software side includes a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface that talks to the motor controller, a Control Application (Python) that issues motion commands, a Logging Application (Python) that continuously records state, and a Human‑Machine Interface (Python) used by the operator. The hardware can operate without the twin, making it a suitable test‑bed for research and education in cyber‑physical systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides a suite of containerised services: • Trajectory Generator Service – uses a kinematic model of the crane to compute optimal movement trajectories that eliminate residual swinging; • Simulation Service – runs a real‑time simulation of the crane and its controllers and can simulate any generated trajectory; • Continuous Validation Service – continuously compares the measured executed trajectory with the simulated one, computes distance metrics, checks against a configurable threshold and notifies the operator via the dashboard; • Dashboard Service – Grafana visualisation of historic and live data stored in the TimescaleDB; • Data Logging Service – via the Logging Application records all crane states to the TimescaleDB; • MQTT Broker – provides lightweight publish/subscribe signalling between services; • Database Service – TimescaleDB stores time‑series data for logging, simulation and validation. These services support monitoring (validation and dashboards), optimisation (trajectory generation), prediction (simulation), control assistance (optimal trajectories) and visualisation for two primary users: the operator (day‑to‑day monitoring and control) and researchers (experimentation, model validation, fault detection).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin is built with open‑source tooling: • Python is used for the Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Human‑Machine Interface and all twin services; • Docker containers orchestrated with Docker‑Compose for one‑click deployment (see Fig. 7); • TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL extension) stores all time‑series data; • Grafana renders dashboards from TimescaleDB; • MQTT broker implements the publish/subscribe communication layer between services; • The kinematic model and CAD data of the crane are provided in the publicly available GitHub repository; • All components are openly released on GitHub and can be run on a standard personal computer.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The exemplar implements a single digital‑twin instance that is composed of six containerised services. The physical crane can operate without the twin, but when the twin is active the services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, Logging, Dashboard, Database) are coordinated centrally via an MQTT broker. No multiple twin instances or decentralized hierarchy are described; responsibility for data acquisition, control, simulation and validation resides within this single orchestrated twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital‑twin follows a container‑based orchestration pattern modelled with the C4 approach. Six Docker containers provide the twin’s core functions: a TimescaleDB database, a Grafana dashboard, a Trajectory Generator service (kinematics model), a Simulation service, a Continuous Validation service, and an MQTT broker for signalling between services. All containers are launched together with a single Docker‑Compose command, establishing a shared network. Python applications (Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Human‑Machine Interface) run in separate containers or on the host, interacting with the crane controllers and the MQTT broker. Resource management relies on Docker container isolation, while persistent time‑series data is stored in TimescaleDB and visualised through Grafana.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with other components uses standard open‑source protocols. Inter‑service communication is performed via MQTT, and data persistence/visualisation rely on TimescaleDB and Grafana accessed through their native APIs. The Python programs communicate with the physical crane’s controllers via a custom interface (not further specified). No external enterprise systems, OPC‑UA, REST, or other interoperability standards are mentioned in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin comprises a CAD model of the gantry crane and a kinematic model used by the Trajectory Generator Service to compute swing‑free movement trajectories. A Simulation Service hosts a physics‑based simulation of the crane and its controllers that can replay generated trajectories. All runtime data (e.g., motor positions, timestamps) are streamed by the Logging Application into a TimescaleDB time‑series database. The data are visualized on a Grafana dashboard and exchanged between services via an MQTT broker. The database acts as the central data store for historical logging, model input, and validation output. The models are linked: the kinematic model feeds both the trajectory generator and the simulation; the simulation output and the logged measurements are consumed by the Continuous Validation Service for comparison.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is provided by a stepper motor that drives the hoist mechanism, controlled by a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller board. The controller is accessed through a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface which communicates with the crane’s low‑level controllers. High‑level commands are issued by the Control Application (Python) and can be invoked remotely via the Human Machine Interface front‑end. The actuation range is limited to the mechanical travel of the hoist and the gantry rails; safety constraints are enforced in software but are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is achieved by using a kinematic model that generates trajectories without residual swing, and a simulation model that reproduces the crane dynamics. Validation is performed continuously by the Continuous Validation Service, which compares logged sensor measurements of executed trajectories with the simulated trajectories, computes distance metrics, and checks them against a predefined threshold. If the threshold is exceeded, the operator is notified through the Grafana dashboard. The approach reuses model validation techniques described in prior publications and is further extended with time‑series classification for fault detection, providing quantitative assurance of model correctness.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data (swing‑angle from the Swing Angle Encoder, wind speed from the Anemometer, motor controller states) are acquired by an Arduino board and transferred to the host PC where the Logging Application continuously records the crane’s state into a TimescaleDB database. Communication between services uses an MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto) for signalling. The stored time‑series data are later consumed by the Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service and visualised on the Grafana dashboard. No explicit transmission frequency, data‑quality checks or preprocessing steps are described, but the Continuous Validation Service performs a post‑hoc comparison of measured trajectories with simulated ones.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application sends movement commands to the Physical Crane Interface, which translates them into controller signals for the cart and hoist motors (Stepper motors driven by TMC4671‑Eval controllers). Commands originate from higher‑level services such as the Trajectory Generator Service (optimal trajectories) and are issued as Python API calls to the interface. Feedback from the crane (position, swing angle) is provided back to the Logging Application and Continuous Validation Service. The documents do not detail command message formats, authentication, or explicit validation before actuation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All software components run on a single personal computer. Docker containers host the TimescaleDB database, the Eclipse Mosquitto MQTT broker and the Grafana dashboard; they communicate over the host’s network via MQTT and PostgreSQL connections. The Python services (Control Application, Logging Application, Simulation Service, etc.) run directly on the host and interact with the containers locally. No specific bandwidth, latency, redundancy or security protocol specifications are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The exemplar is deployed on‑premise on a personal computer. Docker Compose is used to start the TimescaleDB, MQTT broker and Grafana containers automatically; the Python modules are installed with pip (gantrylib). Storage is provided by the TimescaleDB container; computational resources are those of the host PC. The deployment model is a single‑node Docker‑compose setup; scalability beyond this node is not discussed. Availability is supported by Docker Compose’s start‑up scripts that initialize the database and dashboard on first run.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The gantry crane digital twin is engineered as a set of Docker‑compose‑managed containers written in Python. Development starts with physical‑level artefacts (CAD geometry and a kinematic model) that are encapsulated in services: a Trajectory Generator that computes optimal swing‑free movement paths, a Simulation Service that reproduces crane dynamics, and a Continuous Validation Service that repeatedly compares logged sensor measurements against simulated trajectories, computes distance metrics and raises alerts when a configurable threshold is exceeded. All runtime data are persisted in a TimescaleDB instance and visualised through a Grafana dashboard. Inter‑service coordination uses an MQTT broker. The twin is version‑controlled and reproducible via a single `docker compose up` command, enabling researchers to redeploy identical configurations for experiments on continuous validation, fault detection and the DarTwin evolution notation. Evolution is managed by extending or replacing individual services (e.g., adding a fault‑detection classifier) while keeping the same data‑exchange contracts (MQTT topics, database schema).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides decision support through three concrete insight streams. First, the Trajectory Generator Service delivers optimal motion plans that guarantee zero residual swing, which operators can select via the Python‑based Human‑Machine Interface. Second, the Continuous Validation Service continuously evaluates executed trajectories against simulated ones and publishes distance‑metric scores; when the score exceeds a predefined threshold, the Grafana dashboard raises a visual alert, prompting operator intervention. Third, the Simulation Service enables ‘what‑if’ predictions of alternative trajectories, displayed as confidence‑bounded traces (blue simulated, yellow measured) on the dashboard. Users interact through the HMI for manual control and configuration, and through the Grafana UI for monitoring and alert acknowledgement, thereby closing the feedback loop between the physical crane and its digital counterpart.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin

exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

Figure 7 shows that with the single docker compose command the containers and the associated network are up and running. Lastly, fig. 8 shows some traces on the dashboard. Those traces have also been simulated by the simulator, as indicated by the upper (in blue) and lower (in yellow) confidence bounds derived from the simulation. Fig. 8. Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester.

I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and

_A. Architecture_

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give



an overview of the components powering it. We should note
that for this physical system, we take a liberal interpretation
of what goes in a C4 Model component diagram.
Figure 4 shows this component diagram, again for brevity
and readability only the most necessary relationships are
shown. It contains the **Crane Frame** consisting of all the
hardware constituting the physical structure of the crane.
Two **Motors** and associated **Controllers**, one to control the
cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An
**Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up containers,
a **Swing Angle Encoder** measures the swinging motion of
the hoist, and an **Anemometer** measures the wind speed. An
**Arduino** measures and controls those last three components.
Lastly a **PSU** powers the electronics of the crane.


_B. Implementation_


Regarding the implementation, we wanted to keep the
operation of the system as simple as possible, that is, plug
in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of
the software on that computer. Figure 5 shows the distribution
of the C4 Containers visually.
For the implementation, we bundled all the Python C4
Containers in a Python module named _gantrylib_ . To use
this module, it suffices to install it with _pip_ . This way it
remains easy for researchers to implement additional digital
twin services.

**Dashboard** C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A

docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and
down with a single command. For the first time setup, all of
the database and dashboard initialization are also automatically
handled by this docker compose file.

it’s based on PostgreSQL, allowing for commonly known SQL

is what is logged. (iii) it’s open source. For the dashboard, the
choice for Grafana was made because of (i) it’s wide support
for data sources and (ii) again the fact that it’s open source.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**

I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester.

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.
